Complementares — Aula 1

Conteúdos recomendados para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula.

Lesson 1 — The Comeback

Série
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1

Linguagem jurídica e corporativa. Perfeito para o contexto de Rafael como advogado e diretor.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês, preste atenção nas apresentações profissionais.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — 3 Language Tactics to Avoid Being a Me Monster

Episódio sobre como se apresentar e falar de si no trabalho sem monopolizar a conversa: equilibrar falar e ouvir, fazer perguntas e causar boa impressão profissional.

Repita em voz alta uma frase para redirecionar a conversa de volta ao outro.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
TED Talk — Simon Sinek "How Great Leaders Inspire Action"

Vocabulário acessível sobre liderança. Relevante para o contexto de conselheiro.

Dica: anote 3 expressões novas.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — My Company in Detail

Série
Billions — Season 1, Episode 1

Linguagem corporativa de alto nível, finanças e poder. Vocabulário relevante para o contexto de Rafael como diretor.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês, preste atenção em como descrevem empresas e operações.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
ESL Pod — Daily English 181: Company Profiles

Episódio real de business English em que dois profissionais montam o perfil de empresas para uma reunião — vocabulário corporativo (Fortune 500, indústria, faturamento, matriz). Foco no perfil da empresa, não em organograma de departamentos.

Ouça no carro e repita em voz alta as frases que descrevem uma empresa.

Open episode ↗
YouTube
All Ears English — Describing your Company

Vídeo prático sobre como descrever sua empresa em reuniões.

Dica: anote 3 expressões novas e use na próxima aula.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — A Day in Rafael's Life

Série
The Morning Show — Season 1, Episode 1

Rotina matinal intensa de profissionais. Linguagem coloquial americana + vocabulário de rotina.

Dica: preste atenção nas expressões de horário e rotina.

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC) — BOX SET: 'Modern life'

Compilação de episódios curtos sobre a vida moderna do dia a dia — trabalho, tecnologia e hábitos cotidianos — com pronúncia clara. Ótimo para ouvir como as pessoas descrevem suas rotinas.

Anote 3 advérbios de frequência (always, usually, sometimes) que ouvir e escreva uma frase com cada um.

Watch on YouTube ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy — "Daily Routine Vocabulary"

Vocabulário prático para descrever sua rotina diária.

Dica: repita as frases em voz alta, pratique com sua própria rotina.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — First Contact — Meeting Colleagues and Clients

Série
The Good Wife — Season 1, Episode 1

Linguagem juridica e apresentações profissionais em ambiente corporativo. Observe como os personagens se apresentam em reunioes e eventos.

Dica: observe como os personagens se apresentam em reunioes e eventos.

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
Go Natural English — How to Introduce Yourself Fluently (Advanced English Lesson)

Episódio dedicado a como se apresentar com confiança a novos colegas e pessoas que você acabou de conhecer. Você vai ouvir exatamente as estruturas da Aula 1: "I'm...", "I work at...", "Nice to meet you."

Ouça o episódio e depois se apresente em voz alta em 20 segundos, sem ler.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Greg — "Polite English: Can vs Could"

Explicacao clara da diferenca entre Can e Could para pedidos.

Dica: pratique transformando frases informais em formais.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — The Office — Workplace and Team

Série
The Office (US) — Season 1, Episode 1

Vocabulário de escritório de forma humorística. Perfeito para aprender expressões do dia a dia no ambiente de trabalho.

Dica: preste atenção nas descrições do escritório e nos nomes dos objetos.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix, Prime Video e Max

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 299: English Prepositions for the Office

Episódio sobre as preposições de lugar usadas no ambiente de trabalho (in, on, at) — exatamente o que você precisa para descrever seu escritório e onde ficam as coisas.

Repita as preposições em voz alta descrevendo sua própria mesa e sala.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English Addict with Mr Steve — "Office Vocabulary"

Vocabulário completo de escritório com exemplos visuais.

Dica: pause e nomeie os objetos que você vê no seu próprio escritório.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Hotel & Restaurant -- Checking In and Ordering

Série / Filme
"The Grand Budapest Hotel" (2014) -- life inside a hotel

Um filme cheio de cenas de recepção, hóspedes e serviço de hotel. Ótimo para ouvir como funcionários e clientes conversam de forma educada. Conexão com a aula: o vocabulário de hotel em ação.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote 3 frases que os funcionários usam com os hóspedes.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Disney+

Podcast
BBC Learning English — The Vocabulary Show: Travel

Episodio do The Vocabulary Show que ensina 27 palavras e expressoes de viagem (aeroporto, hotel, transporte) em cerca de 12 minutos — pratico para o nivel A2.

Dica: separe as palavras por local (aeroporto, hotel, restaurante) e revise cada grupo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
Much | Many | A little | A few | Some And Any - Countable & Uncountable Nouns in English

Vídeo com diálogos de check-in em hotel e pedidos em restaurante, mostrando o uso de some/any e How much/How many. Revisão direta do foco da aula.

Dica: pause o vídeo e crie 5 frases sobre a sua próxima viagem usando some, any, how much e how many.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Review & Consolidation -- Lessons 7 to 11

Série / Filme
"Up in the Air" (2009) -- a life of business trips

Um executivo que vive viajando a trabalho: aeroportos, hotéis, reuniões e ligações. Perfeito para revisar TODO o vocabulário das aulas 7 a 11 em ação, num contexto profissional real.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase de cada bloco -- e-mail, telefone, números, aeroporto e hotel.

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
BBC Learning English — English at Work: box set episodes 6-10

Compilado da serie English at Work reunindo os episodios 6 a 10, com vocabulario e frases de negocios (telefonemas, e-mails e situacoes de escritorio) para consolidar o basico.

Dica: assista em partes e anote as frases uteis de cada episodio.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
Top 33 Phrasal Verbs for Business English

Vídeo-resumo que percorre os tópicos das aulas 7 a 11: escrever e-mails, fazer chamadas, falar de dados, viajar e se hospedar. Revisão direta de tudo antes de avançar para o passado (Past Simple).

Dica: pause o vídeo e descreva sua próxima viagem de negócios do início ao fim, usando uma frase de cada bloco.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — What Happened? -- Describing Past Events

Série / Filme
"The Founder" (2016) -- how a company grew, step by step

A história de como uma rede cresceu: contratos assinados, lojas abertas, equipes contratadas. Cheio de verbos no passado descrevendo o que a empresa fez -- perfeito para o Past Simple.

Dica: anote cinco verbos regulares no passado (com -ed) que aparecem no filme e descreva as cenas em voz alta.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
BBC Learning English — The past simple tense (6 Minute Grammar)

Episodio de 6 Minute Grammar sobre o Past Simple: verbos regulares com -ed, negativas com 'did not' e perguntas com 'Did', com muitos exemplos falados.

Dica: repita cada exemplo e monte suas proprias frases no passado.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
Pronouncing '-ed' endings - Improve your English with Learners' Questions

Vídeo-aula sobre os três sons da terminação -ed: launched (/t/), signed (/d/), delivered (/d/), wanted (/id/). Treino direto da pronúncia que você praticou nesta aula.

Dica: repita cada exemplo e depois grave a si mesmo descrevendo o trimestre da sua empresa.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Company History -- Milestones and Changes

Série / Filme
"The Social Network" (2010) -- how a company grew and became a giant

A história de como uma empresa foi fundada, cresceu, comprou rivais e se tornou uma das maiores do mundo. Cheio de verbos irregulares no passado (founded, grew, bought, became, won) descrevendo marcos de uma companhia.

Dica: anote cinco verbos IRREGULARES no passado que aparecem no filme e descreva as cenas em voz alta.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
BBC Learning English — How to use irregular verbs (6 Minute Vocabulary)

Episodio de 6 Minute Vocabulary sobre verbos irregulares no passado, mostrando que muitos nao terminam em -ed (grow/grew, buy/bought, win/won) e como usa-los.

Dica: faca uma lista dos irregulares que aparecem e revise em voz alta.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
50 Irregular Past Tense Verbs In English

Vídeo-aula com os verbos irregulares mais comuns e suas formas no passado, incluindo os desta aula: grow-grew, become-became, build-built, buy-bought, win-won. Treino direto das mudanças de forma.

Dica: repita cada par (base → passado) e depois grave a si mesmo contando a história da sua empresa.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Up to Now -- Present Perfect

Série / Filme
"Suits" -- any episode where lawyers report results to the partners

Em reuniões de escritório, os advogados constantemente reportam o que já conseguiram e o que ainda falta: "We have already filed the motion", "We have not heard back yet". Perfeito para ouvir o Present Perfect em contexto jurídico real.

Dica: anote três frases com have/has + participio e marque a palavra de tempo (already, just, yet).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
BBC Learning English — Present perfect with 'just', 'already' and 'yet' (6 Minute Grammar)

Episodio de 6 Minute Grammar sobre o Present Perfect (have/has + participio) com as palavras 'just', 'already' e 'yet', explicando quando usar cada uma.

Dica: pratique frases com 'I've just...', 'I've already...' e 'I haven't... yet'.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
Present Perfect Tense, never, ever, just, already, yet, for, since

Vídeo-aula que compara o Present Perfect (tempo conectado ao agora) com o Past Simple (tempo terminado), exatamente os dois tempos das aulas 13-14 e desta aula. Treino direto das palavras de tempo.

Dica: depois do vídeo, grave-se reportando o que sua empresa já conseguiu este ano usando have/has + participio.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — At the Board Table -- Opinions and Decisions

Série / Filme
"Suits" -- any scene where the partners debate a firm decision

Nas reuniões da Pearson Hardman, os sócios constantemente dão opiniões, discordam e chegam a um acordo: "In my opinion...", "I see your point, but...", "Let us vote". Perfeito para ouvir a linguagem de opinião e decisão em contexto corporativo real.

Dica: anote três frases de opinião e três de concordar/discordar que você ouvir.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
BBC Learning English — How to disagree better (6 Minute English)

Episodio de 6 Minute English que ensina a dar opiniao e discordar de forma respeitosa, com vocabulario para expressar pontos de vista sem ofender.

Dica: anote as frases de concordar/discordar e pratique responder a uma opiniao.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
"Agreeing and Disagreeing in Business English -- meeting phrases"

Vídeo-aula com as frases de uma reunião: como propor (the proposal is...), concordar, discordar com respeito e chegar a um compromise antes de votar. Treino direto do vocabulário desta aula.

Dica: depois do vídeo, grave-se conduzindo uma mini reunião sobre uma decisão da sua empresa.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Legal Basics -- Contracts and Terms

Série / Filme
"Suits" -- any scene where lawyers read or argue over contract terms

Os advogados da série vivem discutindo cláusulas, obrigações e quebras de contrato: "The clause is binding", "They must comply", "We may terminate". Perfeito para ouvir o vocabulário jurídico e os modais shall/must/may em contexto real.

Dica: anote três frases com shall, must ou may e marque se é dever, permissão ou proibição.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
BBC Learning English — 'Have to' and 'must' (6 Minute Grammar)

Episodio de 6 Minute Grammar sobre os modais de obrigacao 'have to' e 'must', explicando a diferenca de uso e como falar de regras e obrigacoes.

Dica: compare 'must' (regra pessoal) e 'have to' (obrigacao externa) com exemplos seus.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
"Legal English: Contract Vocabulary -- clause, party, breach, terminate"

Vídeo-aula de inglês jurídico que apresenta exatamente as palavras desta aula (contract, clause, party, obligation, breach, terminate) e como usá-las ao ler um contrato real.

Dica: depois do vídeo, grave-se explicando três cláusulas de um contrato usando shall, must e may.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Negotiating the Deal -- Offers and Counter-offers

Série / Filme
"Shark Tank" (USA) -- any pitch where entrepreneurs negotiate with investors

No programa, os empreendedores e os investidores fazem ofertas e contrapropostas o tempo todo: "I will give you X if you give me Y percent". Perfeito para ouvir offers, counter-offers e frases com if + will em negociações reais.

Dica: anote três frases com "if... I will..." e veja quem fez a melhor contraproposta.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
BBC Learning English — English at Work: Negotiating a deal (Episode 32)

Episódio da série English at Work em que Anna negocia um acordo com clientes na França — vocabulário de oferta, contraproposta e desconto num contexto real de negócios.

Anote as frases usadas para pechinchar e repita cada uma em voz alta.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
62 Useful Phrases For Negotiating - Business English

Vídeo-aula com as frases exatas desta aula (making an offer, asking for a discount, the first conditional) e como usá-las numa reunião de negócios real.

Dica: depois do vídeo, grave-se fazendo três ofertas condicionais usando if + present, will + verbo.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Closing the Deal -- Finalizing and Reported Speech

Série / Filme
"Suits" -- any scene where Harvey closes a deal and reports it back to the firm

Os advogados fecham acordos e depois relatam ao escritório o que cada lado disse: "He said the deal was off", "She told us she would sign". Perfeito para ouvir vocabulário de fechamento e reported speech em contexto jurídico.

Dica: anote três frases com "he said..." ou "she told me..." e note o recuo do verbo.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
BBC Learning English — English at Work: Clinching the deal (Episode 34)

Episódio 34 de English at Work: a linguagem para fechar (clinch) um acordo durante uma viagem de negócios. Reforça finalize, confirm e expressões para selar o negócio.

Pause após cada fala e repita a frase usada para fechar o acordo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
"Reported Speech in English -- said vs told (with business examples)"

Vídeo-aula com as estruturas exatas desta aula: said sem pessoa, told + pessoa, e o recuo do verbo (is → was, will → would), com exemplos de negócios.

Dica: depois do vídeo, grave-se reportando três frases de uma reunião real usando said e told.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Putting It All Together -- Review & Real-World Practice

Série / Filme
"Suits" -- any full episode where a deal runs from first meeting to signing

Acompanhe um deal completo: a negociação, as regras (must/may), a oferta condicional, o fechamento e o relato de volta ao escritório. É o arco inteiro desta etapa em ação, com vocabulário de stakeholder, scope, milestone e commitment.

Dica: anote uma frase de cada estrutura -- present perfect, opinião, must/may, if + will e reported speech.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
BBC Learning English — English at Work: Box Set Episodes 31–35 (business trip & deal)

Compilação (box set) de English at Work, episódios 31 a 35: acompanha uma viagem de negócios do começo ao fim — planejar, negociar e fechar o acordo, com vocabulário profissional real.

Assista em partes e anote uma expressão nova de cada etapa do processo.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
Learn ALL 16 TENSES Easily in under 30 Minutes - Present, Past, Future, Conditional

Vídeo-aula que revisa, em um só lugar, as cinco estruturas do arco com exemplos de negócios. Perfeito para fixar quando usar cada ferramenta gramatical.

Dica: depois do vídeo, grave-se conduzindo um deal curto usando as cinco estruturas em sequência.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

The Dream
Packing Words
The Code
Getting There
Practice
Your Turn
Wrap-up
T
Lesson 1 — Rafael's First Board Call

The Comeback
Who Is Rafael in English?

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Let's Begin

"How was your week?"
"Tell me about your job in English."
"When do you use English?"
"Tá enferrujado, mas consigo me virar."
— Rafael Gasparello Lima
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1 Business Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2 Present Simple Grammar — routines and facts
3 Introducing Yourself — your first board call
4 Practice — role-play and production
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A group of people who control a company
Board of Directors
A group of people who control a company
"I am a member of the board of directors."
The main office of a company
Headquarters
The main office of a company
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo."
To be in charge of something
To manage
To be in charge of something
"I manage the legal department."
A type of business activity
Industry
A type of business activity
"The iron industry is our main business."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A time when people come together to talk
Meeting
A time when people come together to talk
"I have a meeting every Monday."
A person's job or position
Role
A person's job or position in a company
"My role includes legal duties."
To have someone as your boss
To report to
To have someone as your boss
"I report to the CEO."
A company controlled by a bigger company
Subsidiary
A company controlled by a bigger company
"We have subsidiaries in agriculture."
0 / 4 words revealed
Expressions

Useful Expressions

run a business
administrar um negócio
be in charge of
ser responsável por
work in (an industry)
trabalhar em (um setor)
Vocabulary Review

Say the Word

Board of Directors
Headquarters
To manage
Industry
Meeting
Role
To report to
Subsidiary
Chapter 3: The Code

How to Talk About
Routines and Facts

Grammar Discovery

What Do These Have in Common?

"Rafael works in a large economic group."
"The company operates in two industries."
"He reports to the CEO."
"Rafael trains jiu-jitsu twice a week."
SubjectPresent Simple
I/You/We/Theyverb (base form): I work
He/She/Itverb + s: He works
Negativedon't / doesn't + verb
QuestionDo / Does + subject + verb?
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

Rafael's actual error from the consultation

"When I woke up, I take my son to school."
"When I wake up, I take my son to school."

Use Present Simple for daily routines — not Past Simple!

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentences

"Rafael ___works at Grupo Picunha." (fact)
"The company ___operates in iron and agriculture." (fact)
"He ___doesn't use English at work every day." (negative)
"___Does he travel to the U.S. every year?" (question)
Grammar Focus

Negatives and Questions

Negative: "I don't work on weekends."
Question: "Does your company have subsidiaries?"
Negative: "He doesn't report to the board."
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"Rafael work in São Paulo."
"Rafael works in São Paulo." (he + verb+s)
"He don't travel overseas."
"He doesn't travel overseas." (doesn't for he/she/it)
"Does the company operates in agriculture?"
"Does the company operate in agriculture?" (base form after does)
0 / 3
Chapter 4: Getting There

Rafael's First
Board Call

Dialogue

The Board Call

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

What does Rafael's company do?
It operates in iron manufacturing and agriculture.
What is Rafael's role?
He manages the legal department and serves on the board of directors.
How often does Rafael travel to the U.S.?
Once a year.
Company Profile

Rafael's Card

Rafael Gasparello Lima
Lawyer, Director & Board Member
Industries: Iron Manufacturing | Agriculture
Headquarters: São Paulo, Brazil
Reports to: CEO
GRUPO PICUNHA
Listening

Listen First

A professional self-introduction

0:000:00
Speed
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
You arrive at a conference. A person says: "Hi, what do you do?" Your answer:
"I am a lawyer and director at Grupo Picunha. We operate in the iron industry and agriculture."
1 / 6
Quick Fire

Keep Going

0 / 6
Your boss asks: "Do you use English at work?" Your answer:
"Not very often, but I want to improve. I am taking English lessons."
1 / 6

6/6 — Excellent!

You are ready for the next challenge.

Guided Role-play

Join a Video Call

Guided: Use the keywords

You are joining a video call with an international team for the first time. Introduce yourself using the keywords below.
introduce yourselfcompanyroleindustriesheadquarters
Semi-free Role-play

Now Without the Script

Semi-free: Only keywords

Now do it again, but without the script. Use only the keywords to help you.
namelawyerGrupo PicunhaironagricultureboardCEO
Free Role-play

Meet an American Investor

Free: No support — just talk!

Your CEO introduces you to an American investor at dinner. Introduce yourself naturally. No keywords, no script — just you.
Survival Card

5 Phrases to Survive

1
My name is Rafael Gasparello Lima. I am a lawyer and director.
2
I work at Grupo Picunha. We operate in iron and agriculture.
3
Our headquarters is in São Paulo.
4
I report to the CEO and serve on the board of directors.
5
Could you repeat that, please?
What I Learned

Lesson 1 Checklist

I can introduce myself in English
I can describe my company
I can use Present Simple for routines
I know the difference between "wake" and "woke"
I can ask someone to repeat

Lesson 1 — Complete

Next: Lesson 2 — "My Company in Detail"

Homework: Record a 1-minute audio introducing yourself and your company.

Lesson 2 — Inside Grupo Picunha

My Company in Detail
Describing Your Company in English

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Callback — Lesson 1

Do You Remember?

Say the word from Lesson 1

"The main office of a company" → ___Headquarters
"A group of people who control a company" → ___Board of Directors
"To be in charge of something" → ___To manage
"A person's job or position" → ___Role
Chapter 1: The Dream

Tell Me About Your Company

"Tell me about Grupo Picunha in English."
"How many people work there?"
"What does the company produce?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1 Company Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2 3rd Person Present Simple — he/she/it rules
3 Describing Operations — the investor call
4 Practice — role-play and production
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A part of a company that does a specific job
Department
A part of a company that does a specific job
"Rafael works in the legal department."
To give something that is needed
To supply
To give something that is needed
"Our company supplies iron to other businesses."
The process of making products in a factory
Manufacturing
The process of making products in a factory
"The iron manufacturing division is the biggest."
The money a company earns from business
Revenue
The money a company earns from business
"Our revenue comes from two main industries."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A person who works for a company
Employee
A person who works for a company
"The group has over five hundred employees."
A person or company that buys your services
Client
A person or company that buys your services
"We have clients in Brazil and overseas."
A business relationship between companies
Partnership
A business relationship between companies
"We have partnerships with international firms."
To grow bigger or increase in size
To expand
To grow bigger or increase in size
"The company wants to expand operations overseas."
0 / 4 words revealed
Expressions

Useful Expressions

be based in
"Our company is based in São Paulo."
focus on
"The legal department focuses on contracts."
deal with
"I deal with corporate governance issues."
Vocabulary Review

Say the Word

Department
To supply
Manufacturing
Revenue
Employee
Client
Partnership
To expand
Chapter 3: The Code

Third Person
He, She, It

Grammar Discovery

What Do You Notice?

"The company manufactures iron products."
"Rafael deals with corporate governance."
"The division produces fruits and meat."
"The group has over five hundred employees."

What happens to the verb when we talk about he/she/it/the company?

RuleExample
Most verbs: add -swork → works, operate → operates
-ch/-sh/-s/-x/-o: add -esgo → goes, do → does
Consonant + y: -iessupply → supplies
Irregular: have → hasThe company has 500 employees
Spelling Rules

3rd Person Spelling

Most verbs: work → works  |  operate → operates
-ch / -sh / -s / -x / -o: go → goes  |  do → does
Consonant + y: supply → supplies  |  study → studies
Irregular: have → has
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"The company supply iron."
"The company supplies iron."
"The group have many employees."
"The group has many employees."
Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentences

"The company ___manufactures iron products."
"Our division ___produces fruits and meat."
"The CEO ___doesn't want to wait." (negative)
"___Does the company ___have international clients?"
Grammar Focus

Negatives and Questions

Negative: "The company doesn't export directly."
Question: "Does the company have international clients?"
Negative: "Rafael doesn't work in the agriculture division."
Question: "Does the legal department focus on contracts?"
Chapter 4: Getting There

Describing Your Company
To the World

Dialogue

The Investor Call

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

What does the first division do?
It manufactures and supplies iron.
How many employees does the group have?
Over five hundred.
Does the company export directly?
No, but it has international partnerships.
Listening

Listen First

A company description

0:000:00
Speed
Company Fact Sheet

Grupo Picunha

GRUPO PICUNHA
Headquarters:São Paulo, Brazil Divisions:Iron Manufacturing | Agriculture Employees:500+ Products:Iron, Fruits, Meat Key Markets:Brazil (domestic), expanding internationally Legal Director:Rafael Gasparello Lima
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
An investor asks: "What does your company produce?" Your answer:
"Our company manufactures iron and produces fruits and meat for domestic and international markets."
1 / 6
Quick Fire

Keep Going

0 / 6
Your CEO asks you to describe the iron division. Your answer:
"Our first division manufactures and supplies iron to construction companies across Brazil."
1 / 6
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"The company supply iron to clients."
"The company supplies iron to clients." (supply → supplies)
"The group have many employees."
"The group has many employees." (have → has)
"Does the division produces meat?"
"Does the division produce meat?" (base form after does)
0 / 3

Excellent Work!

You are ready for the next challenge.

Guided Role-play

Industry Conference

Guided: Use the keywords

You are presenting Grupo Picunha at an industry conference. Describe your company using the keywords below.
based inoperatesmanufacturessuppliesemployeespartnershipsexpand
Semi-free Role-play

Now Without the Script

Semi-free: Only keywords

Describe your company again, but this time with only the keywords to help you.
São Pauloironagriculture500+internationaloverseas
Free Role-play

A Journalist Asks

Free: No support — just talk!

A journalist asks you about your company for a business magazine article. Describe it naturally. No keywords, no script — just you.
Survival Card

5 Phrases to Survive

1
Our company operates in two industries: iron and agriculture.
2
The group has over five hundred employees.
3
We supply iron to construction companies across Brazil.
4
We have partnerships with international firms.
5
The company wants to expand operations overseas.
What I Learned

Lesson 2 Checklist

I can describe my company's operations
I can use Present Simple with he/she/it correctly
I know the -s/-es/-ies rules
I can answer questions about my company
I can describe company structure

Lesson 2 — Complete

Next: Lesson 3 — "Daily Routine"

Homework: Write a short paragraph (5-6 sentences) describing Grupo Picunha. Use the vocabulary from today. Send to your teacher.

Lesson 3 — A Day in Rafael's Life

Daily Routine
A Day in Rafael's Life

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Callback — Lessons 1 & 2

Do You Remember?

Complete with words from previous lessons

"Our company ___operates in two ___industries."
"The group ___has over five hundred ___employees."
Chapter 1: The Dream

Your Morning

"What time do you wake up?"
"What do you do before work?"
"Do you exercise?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1 Routine Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2 Frequency Adverbs — always, usually, never
3 Describing Your Day — coffee break dialogue
4 Practice — role-play and production
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

The trip from home to work
Commute
The trip from home to work
"Rafael's commute takes about thirty minutes."
A plan for when things will happen
Schedule
A plan for when things will happen
"My schedule is very busy during the week."
The last day to finish something
Deadline
The last day to finish something
"I have an important deadline on Friday."
The time during work to eat lunch
Lunch break
The time during work to eat lunch
"I usually have lunch during my lunch break at 12."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To go to a meeting or event
To attend
To go to a meeting or event
"I attend meetings every morning."
The things you do every day in the same order
Routine
The things you do every day in the same order
"My routine starts at 6 a.m."
A planned meeting at a specific time
Appointment
A planned meeting at a specific time
"I have an appointment with a client at 3 p.m."
Extra time worked after normal hours
Overtime
Extra time worked after normal hours
"I rarely work overtime, but this week is different."
0 / 4 words revealed
Expressions

Useful Expressions

wake up early
"I always wake up early to take my son to school."
work out
"Rafael works out twice a week. He trains jiu-jitsu."
pick up (someone)
"I pick up my son from school at 5 p.m."
Vocabulary Check

Say the Word

I say the definition, you say the word

"The trip from home to work" → ___Commute
"Extra time worked after normal hours" → ___Overtime
"A plan for when things will happen" → ___Schedule
"The last day to finish something" → ___Deadline
Chapter 3

The Code
How often do you...?

Grammar Discovery

Find the Pattern

Where are the blue words? Before or after the verb?

Rafael always wakes up at 6 a.m.
He usually takes his son to school.
He sometimes trains jiu-jitsu in the evening.
He never works on weekends.
AdverbFrequencyExample
always100%I always wake up early.
usually80%I usually have lunch at 12.
often60%He often attends meetings.
sometimes40%I sometimes work late.
rarely10%She rarely works overtime.
never0%I never work on weekends.
Position Rule

Where Does It Go?

I always wake up early. (before main verb)
He is always on time. (after TO BE)
Always I wake up early. (WRONG)
Common Mistake

Watch Out

"When I woke up, I take my son to school."
"When I wake up, I always take my son to school."
"Usually I go to work at 8."
"I usually go to work at 8."
Practice

Fill in the Adverb

"I ___always wake up early."
"He ___usually has lunch at 12."
"She ___rarely works overtime."
"He is ___never late for meetings."
Frequency Scale

How Often?

100%always
80%usually
60%often
40%sometimes
10%rarely
0%never
Chapter 4

Getting There
From Sunrise to Sunset

Dialogue

Coffee Break with Mark

Comprehension

Did You Understand?

1. What time does Rafael wake up?
He always wakes up at 6 a.m.
2. How often does Rafael train jiu-jitsu?
Twice a week.
3. Does Rafael work on weekends?
No, he never works on weekends.
Listening

Listen Carefully

Listen to the routine. How many frequency adverbs do you hear?

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does Rafael always do at 6 a.m.?
He wakes up and takes his son to school.
2. How often does he work overtime?
He rarely works overtime.
Your Week

Rafael's Weekly Planner

TIME MONDAY TUESDAY WEDNESDAY
6:00Wake upWake upWake up
6:30Son → SchoolSon → SchoolSon → School
7:00Jiu-jitsu—Jiu-jitsu
8:30LunchLunchLunch
9:00OfficeOfficeOffice
9:30MeetingsLegal workMeetings
12:00Lunch breakLunch breakLunch break
13:00MeetingsBoard prepClient calls
18:00Pick up sonPick up sonPick up son
20:00English classJiu-jitsuEnglish class
How often does Rafael train jiu-jitsu?
He often trains jiu-jitsu (3 times: Mon morning, Tue evening, Wed morning).
What does Rafael always do at 6:30?
He always takes his son to school.
Quick Fire
0 / 6
1 / 6
Your colleague asks: "What do you usually do in the morning?" Answer:
"I usually wake up at 6, take my son to school, and then train jiu-jitsu."
Quick Fire
0 / 6
1 / 6
A friend asks: "What is your commute like?" Answer:
"My commute takes about thirty minutes. I usually drive to the office."
Spot the Error

Fix the Mistake

"Always I wake up at 6 a.m."
"I always wake up at 6 a.m."
"He never is late for meetings."
"He is never late for meetings."
"I work overtime rarely."
"I rarely work overtime."
0 / 3

Practice Complete

Great job with frequency adverbs!

Your Turn — Guided

Your new American colleague wants to know about your daily routine.

Describe your day from morning to evening. Follow this order: morning → commute → work → evening
wake up commute schedule attend meetings lunch break pick up work out
Your Turn — Semi-Free

Describe your day using only these time anchors

No script this time. Use the times below to structure your answer. Include frequency adverbs.
6:00 AM 9:00 AM 12:00 PM 6:00 PM 8:00 PM
Your Turn — Free

A journalist is writing about successful executives

Describe your typical day naturally. The journalist wants to know what a day in your life looks like. Speak freely — no keywords, no script.
Survival Card

Key Phrases

1
I always wake up early and take my son to school.
2
My schedule is very busy during the week.
3
I usually attend meetings in the morning.
4
I rarely work overtime.
5
I never work on weekends.
What I Learned

Lesson 3 Checklist

I can describe my daily routine
I can use frequency adverbs correctly
I know where to put always/usually/never
I can talk about my schedule and commute
I know the difference between wake/woke

Lesson 3 — Complete

Next: Lesson 4 — "First Contact"

Homework: Record a 2-minute audio describing your daily routine. Use at least 4 frequency adverbs.

Lesson 4 — First Contact

First Contact
Meeting New People in English

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Review

"Describe your morning routine using frequency adverbs."
"What do you always do before work?"
"How often do you attend meetings?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Let's Talk

"Have you ever met someone important at a conference?"
"What do you say when you meet someone new?"
"Do you have a business card?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1 Meeting Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2 Can/Could for Requests — polite language
3 First Impressions — networking dialogue
4 Practice — role-play and production
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A person you work with
Colleague
A person you work with
"I have a meeting with my colleague from the legal team."
To tell someone another person's name
To introduce
To tell someone another person's name
"Let me introduce you to our CEO."
A polite way to greet someone new
Pleased to meet you
A polite way to greet someone new
"Pleased to meet you. I am Rafael."
A small card with your contact information
Business card
A small card with your contact information
"Here is my business card."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To hold and move someone's hand when you meet
To shake hands
To hold and move someone's hand when you meet
"In business, we always shake hands."
Free to do something or meet someone
Available
Free to do something or meet someone
"Are you available for a meeting tomorrow?"
To plan or organize something
To arrange
To plan or organize something
"Can we arrange a meeting for next week?"
Phone number, email, and address
Contact information
Phone number, email, and address
"Could you send me your contact information?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Expressions

Useful Expressions

Nice to meet you
"Nice to meet you. I have heard great things about your work."
looking forward to
"I am looking forward to working with you."
get in touch
"Let us get in touch next week to discuss the project."
Vocabulary Check

Match the Definition

1. A person you work with
Colleague
2. A small card with your contact information
Business card
3. Free to do something or meet someone
Available
4. To plan or organize something
To arrange
Chapter 3

The Code
The Art of Polite Requests

Grammar Discovery

What Do You Notice?

1. "Can I ask what company you are with?"
2. "Could you tell me about your company?"
3. "Can we arrange a meeting?"
4. "Could I give you my business card?"

What is the difference between CAN and COULD?

Click to reveal the rule
Can = informal, everyday. Could = more polite, professional. Both + base verb (no "to").
Grammar

Can / Could — 4 Uses

Use Can (informal) Could (more polite)
RequestCan you repeat that?Could you repeat that, please?
OfferCan I help you?Could I give you my card?
AbilityI can speak English.I could understand him yesterday.
PermissionCan I sit here?Could I ask you a question?
Common Mistake

Watch Out

✗ "Could you to send me an email?"
✓ "Could you send me an email?"
✗ "Can you sending me your card?"
✓ "Can you send me your card?"

Rule: Can/Could + base verb (NO "to", NO "-ing")

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentence

1. "_____ you repeat that, please?"
Could
2. "_____ I help you with something?"
Can
3. "I _____ speak English."
can
4. "_____ we arrange a meeting?"
Could
Formality Scale

Informal → Formal

DIRECT (TOO INFORMAL)
"Send me your email."
INFORMAL (OK WITH COLLEAGUES)
"Can you send me your email?"
POLITE (PROFESSIONAL)
"Could you send me your email, please?"
Chapter 4

Getting There
Your First Impression

Dialogue

At the Conference with Karen

Comprehension

Did You Understand?

1. What does Rafael offer Karen?
His business card.
2. What does Karen ask Rafael to do?
Send her an email with his contact information.
3. When do they plan to meet?
Tuesday morning next week.
Listening

Listen Carefully

Two people meeting at a conference.

0:000:00
Speed
1. How many times did you hear CAN or COULD?
At least 6 times.
2. What did Rafael offer?
His business card.
Your Business Card

Read Your Card

GRUPO PICUNHA
Rafael Gasparello Lima
Lawyer, Director & Board Member
rafael@grupopicunha.com.br
+55 11 9XXXX-XXXX
São Paulo, Brazil
Could you read your business card to Karen?
Practice: "My name is Rafael Gasparello Lima. I am a Lawyer, Director, and Board Member at Grupo Picunha..."
Quick Fire
0 / 6
1 / 6
You want to politely ask someone their name. What do you say?
"Could I ask what your name is?" or "Could you tell me your name?"
Quick Fire
0 / 6
1 / 6
You want someone to send you an email. What do you say?
"Could you send me an email with your contact information?"
Spot the Error

Fix the Mistake

"Could you to tell me your name?"
"Could you tell me your name?"
"Can you sending me an email?"
"Can you send me an email?"
"I am look forward to work with you."
"I am looking forward to working with you."
0 / 3

Practice Complete

Great job with Can and Could!

Your Turn — Guided

You are at a business conference in Miami. You meet an American lawyer.

Introduce yourself, ask about their company, offer your business card, and arrange a future meeting.
pleased to meet you business card could you can we arrange looking forward to get in touch
Your Turn — Semi-Free

Same scenario, but no script this time

Use only these keywords to structure your conversation. Include both Can and Could.
introduce business card arrange contact information
Your Turn — Free

You meet the CEO of an American company at a dinner

Make a strong first impression. Introduce yourself, learn about their company, exchange contact information, and arrange a follow-up. Speak freely — no keywords, no script.
Survival Card

Key Phrases

1
Pleased to meet you. I am Rafael Gasparello Lima.
2
Could I give you my business card?
3
Could you tell me more about your company?
4
Can we arrange a meeting for next week?
5
I am looking forward to working with you.
What I Learned

Lesson 4 Checklist

I can introduce myself professionally
I can use Can and Could for requests
I know when to use Could instead of Can
I can arrange a meeting in English
I can exchange business cards politely

Lesson 4 — Complete

Next: Lesson 5 — "The Office"

Homework: Write 5 sentences using Can and Could about your work.

Lesson 5 — A Tour of Grupo Picunha

A Tour of Grupo Picunha
The Office — Workplace and Team

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Review

"Introduce yourself to me using Can or Could."
"Pleased to meet you. Could I give you my business card?"
"Can we arrange a meeting for next week?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Let's Talk

"Describe your office."
"What floor is your department on?"
"What is near your desk?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1 Office Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2 There is / There are — describing what exists
3 Giving a Tour — office dialogue
4 Practice — role-play and production
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

The place where you work
Office
The place where you work
"My office is on the third floor."
A large room for meetings
Conference room
A large room for meetings
"There is a conference room on every floor."
A level of a building
Floor
A level of a building
"The legal department is on the fifth floor."
A machine that prints documents
Printer
A machine that prints documents
"There is a printer next to my desk."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

The area where visitors arrive
Reception
The area where visitors arrive
"The reception is on the ground floor."
A place to park your car
Parking lot
A place to park your car
"There is a parking lot behind the building."
A machine that moves people between floors
Elevator
A machine that moves people between floors
"The elevator is next to the stairs."
A place in a building where you can eat
Cafeteria
A place in a building where you can eat
"There is a cafeteria on the first floor."
0 / 4 words revealed
Expressions

Useful Expressions

on the (first) floor
"The legal department is on the fifth floor."
next to
"My office is next to the conference room."
in front of
"The reception desk is in front of the elevator."
Vocabulary Check

Match the Definition

1. The place where you work
Office
2. A machine that moves people between floors
Elevator
3. A place to park your car
Parking lot
4. A place in a building where you can eat
Cafeteria
Chapter 3

The Code
What Is in Your Building?

Grammar Discovery

What Do You Notice?

1. "There is a conference room on every floor."
2. "There are three meeting rooms on my floor."
3. "There isn't an international office yet."
4. "Are there any cafeterias in the building?"

When do we use IS and when do we use ARE?

Click to reveal the rule
Singular = There is. Plural = There are. In Portuguese we say "Tem" for both, but English changes the verb.
Grammar

There is / There are — Full Table

Singular Plural
AffirmativeThere is a conference room.There are three meeting rooms.
NegativeThere isn't an office overseas.There aren't any problems.
QuestionIs there a cafeteria? Yes, there is.Are there any meeting rooms? Yes, there are.
Grammar

Prepositions of Place

inThe printer is in the office.
onMy office is on the fifth floor.
atI am at the reception.
next toThe elevator is next to the stairs.
betweenThe cafeteria is between the lobby and the parking lot.
in front ofThe desk is in front of the window.
behindThe parking lot is behind the building.
Common Mistake

Watch Out

✗ "It has a conference room on this floor."
✓ "There is a conference room on this floor."
✗ "There is many employees."
✓ "There are many employees."

Rule: Singular = There is / Plural = There are. Never use "It has" to describe existence.

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentence

1. "There _____ a cafeteria on the first floor."
is
2. "There _____ many employees in the building."
are
3. "_____ there a parking lot? Yes, there _____."
Is / is
4. "There _____ any international offices yet."
aren't
Chapter 4

Getting There
Welcome to Our Headquarters

Dialogue

The Office Tour with Tom

Comprehension

Did You Understand?

1. How many floors are there?
There are ten floors.
2. Where is the cafeteria?
On the first floor, next to the lobby.
3. Where is the parking lot?
Behind the building.
Listening

Listen Carefully

Listen to the office description. How many times do you hear THERE IS or THERE ARE?

0:000:00
Speed
1. Where is Rafael's office?
On the fifth floor.
2. What is next to his office?
A conference room.
Your Floor

Rafael's Floor Plan — 5th Floor

Meeting Room 1
Meeting Room 2
Meeting Room 3
Rafael's Office
Conference Room
Bathroom
Elevator
Printer
Stairs
Where is the conference room?
The conference room is next to Rafael's office.
Where is the printer?
The printer is in front of the elevator.
Quick Fire
0 / 6
1 / 6
A visitor asks: "Is there a coffee machine on this floor?" Answer:
"Yes, there is. The coffee machine is in the cafeteria on the first floor."
Quick Fire
0 / 6
1 / 6
Describe where the parking lot is.
"There is a large parking lot behind the building."
Spot the Error

Fix the Mistake

"It has a conference room on this floor."
"There is a conference room on this floor."
"There is many employees in the building."
"There are many employees in the building."
"The cafeteria is in the first floor."
"The cafeteria is on the first floor."
0 / 3

Practice Complete

Great job with There is/There are!

Your Turn — Guided

A new international employee is visiting. Give them a tour of your building.

Welcome them, show the reception, take them to the elevator, describe your floor, show the conference room, and mention the cafeteria and parking lot.
there is there are on the (floor) next to behind in front of reception cafeteria conference room
Your Turn — Semi-Free

Same scenario, but no script this time

Use only these preposition keywords to describe your building.
on next to behind in front of between
Your Turn — Free

A real estate agent is showing you a new office space

Ask questions using Is there / Are there. Find out about the conference rooms, parking lot, cafeteria, and elevator. Speak freely — no keywords, no script.
Survival Card

Key Phrases

1
My office is on the fifth floor.
2
There is a conference room next to my office.
3
There are three meeting rooms on this floor.
4
The cafeteria is on the first floor.
5
Is there a parking lot? Yes, it is behind the building.
What I Learned

Lesson 5 Checklist

I can describe my office building
I can use There is and There are correctly
I know 7 prepositions of place
I can give an office tour in English
I know the difference between There is and It has

Lesson 5 — Complete

Next: Lesson 6 — "Review and Consolidation"

Homework: Draw a simple map of your office floor. Label 5 locations in English. Write 5 sentences using There is/There are.

Lesson 6 — Hosting an International Partner

Putting It All
Together

Review & Consolidation — Lessons 1-5 — A2 — 60 minutes

Chapter 1: The Dream

Do You Remember?

"Introduce yourself: who are you and what do you do?"
"Describe your office — what is on your floor?"
"What do you usually do before work?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1 Vocabulary Review — words from all five lessons
2 Grammar Recap — Present Simple, frequency, Can/Could, There is/are
3 Hosting a Partner — one conversation, everything you learned
4 Free Practice — show what you can do
Vocabulary Review

Words You Know — 1-4

The main office of a company (Lesson 1)
Headquarters
The main office of a company
"Our headquarters is in São Paulo."
A company controlled by a bigger company (Lesson 1)
Subsidiary
A company controlled by a bigger company
"We have subsidiaries in agriculture."
The money a company earns (Lesson 2)
Revenue
The money a company earns
"Our revenue comes from two divisions."
A business relationship between companies (Lesson 2)
Partnership
A business relationship between companies
"We have partnerships with international firms."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Words You Know — 5-8

Your plan of activities and times (Lesson 3)
Schedule
Your plan of activities and times
"My schedule is very busy during the week."
Your trip from home to work (Lesson 3)
Commute
Your trip from home to work
"My commute takes about thirty minutes."
A room for meetings (Lesson 5)
Conference room
A room where people have meetings
"There is a conference room next to my office."
An area where cars are parked (Lesson 5)
Parking lot
An area where people park their cars
"There is a large parking lot behind the building."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Use It in a Sentence

Headquarters
Subsidiary
Revenue
Partnership
Schedule
Commute
Conference room
Parking lot
Chapter 3: The Code

Four Tools,
One Toolbox

Grammar Recap 1

Routines & Facts

"Rafael works at Grupo Picunha."
"The company operates in two industries."
"He always wakes up early."
"I rarely work overtime."
UseForm
I/You/We/Theyverb (base form): I work
He/She/Itverb + s: He works
Frequency adverbbefore the verb: I always work / He rarely works
Negative / Questiondon't / doesn't + verb · Do / Does + verb?
Grammar Recap 2

Requests & Descriptions

ToolUseExample
Canability / informal request"Can we arrange a meeting?"
Couldpolite request / offer"Could you send me an email?"
There isone thing (singular)"There is a conference room."
There aremore things (plural)"There are three meeting rooms."
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"When I woke up, I take my son to school."
"When I wake up, I take my son to school."
"Does the company operates in agriculture?"
"Does the company operate in agriculture?"

Present Simple for routines · base form after "does"

Grammar Practice

Complete the Sentences

"Our company ___operates in two industries." (fact)
"I ___usually attend meetings in the morning." (frequency)
"___Could you send me an email, please?" (polite request)
"___There are three meeting rooms on this floor." (plural)
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"He don't work on weekends."
"He doesn't work on weekends." (doesn't for he/she/it)
"There is three meeting rooms on my floor."
"There are three meeting rooms on my floor." (plural → there are)
"Can you to send me the report?"
"Can you send me the report?" (no 'to' after can/could)
"I always am late for the commute."
"I am always late for the commute." (frequency adverb after 'to be')
0 / 4
Chapter 4: Getting There

A Partner
Visits the Office

Dialogue

Hosting Sarah

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

What industries does Rafael's company operate in?
Iron manufacturing and agriculture.
What does Rafael usually do in the morning?
He wakes up early, takes his son to school, and attends meetings.
Where is the conference room?
Next to his office, on the fifth floor.
Visit Itinerary

Sarah's Day at Picunha

Visitor Itinerary
Sarah Collins — Williams & Associates
09:00 — Welcome & company overview (5th floor)
10:30 — Meeting with the legal team (conference room)
12:30 — Lunch (cafeteria, 1st floor)
14:00 — Office tour & next steps
GRUPO PICUNHA
Listening 1

A Company Overview

Listen first — no text. What does the company do?

0:000:00
Speed
Listening 2

An Office Tour

Listen first — where is everything?

0:000:00
Speed
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
A partner asks: "What does your company do?" Your answer:
"We operate in two industries: iron manufacturing and agriculture. Our revenue comes from both divisions."
1 / 6
Quick Fire

Keep Going

0 / 6
A visitor asks: "Is there a parking lot?" Your answer:
"Yes, there is. There is a large parking lot behind the building."
1 / 6

12/12 — Excellent!

Everything from Lessons 1-5, working together.

Guided Role-play

Welcome a Partner

Guided: Use the keywords

An international partner arrives at your headquarters. Welcome her, introduce yourself, and describe your company using the keywords below.
welcomedirectortwo industriesheadquartersrevenue
Semi-free Role-play

Arrange & Show Around

Semi-free: Only keywords

Now arrange an afternoon meeting and give the partner a short office tour. Use only the keywords.
Could youarrange a meetingthere isthere arefifth floorparking lot
Free Role-play

Host the Whole Visit

Free: No support — just host!

Your CEO asks you to host an American partner for the day. Welcome her, describe your company and your routine, arrange a meeting, and give a tour of the office. No keywords, no script — just you.
Your Toolkit

5 Sentences That Carry It All

1
I am a lawyer and director at Grupo Picunha. We operate in iron and agriculture.
2
Our headquarters is in São Paulo, and our revenue comes from two divisions.
3
I usually wake up early and attend meetings all morning.
4
Could you send me the topics by email?
5
There is a conference room next to my office on the fifth floor.
What I Learned

Lesson 6 Checklist

I can introduce myself and describe my company
I can describe my routine with frequency adverbs
I can make polite requests with Can and Could
I can describe my office with There is / There are
I can use Present Simple correctly for routines and facts

Lessons 1-5 — Consolidated

Next: Lesson 7 — "Business Emails: Writing Clear Messages"

Homework: Record a 2-minute audio hosting an international partner.

Lesson 7 — Rafael's Inbox

Business
Emails

Writing Clear Messages — A2 — 60 minutes

Chapter 1: The Dream

From Speaking to Writing

"Last time: 'Could you send me the topics by email?' — how do we write it?"
"How many emails do you write in English each week?"
"Who do you usually write to — clients, your team, partners?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1Email Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2Openings, Closings & Polite Requests
3Write a Real Email to a Partner
4Practice — compose and reply
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A file you send with an email
Attachment
A file you send with an email
"Please find the report in the attachment."
The person who receives the email
Recipient
The person who receives the email
"Add the recipient before you send it."
The title of an email
Subject line
The title that says what the email is about
"Write 'Contract Review' in the subject line."
To answer an email
To reply
To answer an email
"I will reply to your message today."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To send a received email to someone else
To forward
To send a received email to someone else
"I will forward it to my legal team."
A polite word to end an email
Regards
A polite word to close an email
"Best regards, Rafael."
A version of an email before you send it
Draft
A version of an email before you send it
"I will write a draft and check it first."
To send another message to check progress
To follow up
To send another message to check progress
"I will follow up next week."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Use It in a Sentence

Attachment
Recipient
Subject line
To reply
To forward
Regards
Draft
To follow up
Chapter 3: The Code

The Shape of a
Good Email

Grammar Discovery

Opening & Closing

"Dear Ms. Collins,"
"I am writing to share my comments."
"I look forward to your reply."
"Best regards, Rafael"
PartPhrase
GreetingDear Mr./Ms. + last name,
ReasonI am writing to + verb...
Polite closeI look forward to your reply.
Sign-offBest regards, + your name
Grammar Recap

Polite Imperatives

FunctionEmail phrase
Send a file"Please find the report attached."
Invite questions"Please let me know if you have questions."
Polite request"Could you send me the contract?"
Formal > informal"I am writing to..." (not "Hey, quick thing")
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I wait your reply."
"I look forward to your reply."
"I send you the file in attachment."
"Please find the file attached."

Use fixed email phrases — not word-for-word translation.

Grammar Practice

Complete the Email

"___Dear Ms. Collins," (greeting)
"I am ___writing to share my comments." (reason)
"Please find the report ___attached." (send a file)
"Best ___regards, Rafael" (sign-off)
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"I wait your answer soon."
"I look forward to your reply." (fixed email phrase)
"Please to send me the contract."
"Please send me the contract." (no 'to' after please + imperative)
"Dear Sarah, can you send the file? Thx."
"Dear Ms. Collins, could you send the file? Best regards." (formal register)
"I forward you the email to my team."
"I will forward the email to my team." (will + base verb)
0 / 4
Chapter 4: Getting There

Talking About
an Email

Dialogue

Coordinating by Email

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

What did Sarah's email include?
An attachment about the contract.
What does Rafael ask Sarah to write in the subject line?
"Contract Review".
When will Rafael follow up?
Next week.
A Real Email

Rafael's Reply

New Message
To: Sarah Collins — Williams & Associates
Subject: Contract Review — Comments
Dear Ms. Collins,
Thank you for your email. Please find my comments attached.
Could you forward this to your team? I look forward to your reply.
Best regards, Rafael
Listening 1

A Voicemail from Sarah

Reading the Email

Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
You received an email with a file. How do you thank the sender?

Keep Going

0 / 6
You need more time to answer. What do you write?

Clear subject, polite request, professional close.

Guided Role-play

Dictate an Email

Guided: Use the keywords

Dictate a short email to your teacher: reply to a partner who sent a contract. Use the keywords below.
Dear Ms. Collinsthank youattachedcould youbest regards
Semi-free Role-play

Write a Follow-up

Semi-free: Only keywords

Now dictate a follow-up email: you have not received a reply yet. Use only the keywords.
follow upsubject lineforwardrecipientlet me know
Free Role-play

Your Own Email

Free: No support — just write!

Dictate a complete email to an American partner: introduce yourself, explain why you are writing, make one request, and close professionally. No keywords — just you.
Your Toolkit

5 Email Phrases You Always Need

1
Dear Ms. Collins, I am writing to share my comments.
2
Please find the report attached.
3
Could you reply by Friday, please?
4
Please let me know if you have any questions.
5
I look forward to your reply. Best regards, Rafael.
What I Learned

Lesson 7 Checklist

I can open an email with a formal greeting
I can say why I am writing
I can attach a file and make a polite request
I can close an email professionally
I can write a follow-up message

Lesson 7 — Complete

Next: Lesson 8 — "On the Phone: Calls and Video Conferences"

Homework: Write one real work email in English this week.

Lesson 8 — Rafael Takes the Call

On the
Phone

Calls & Video Conferences — A2 — 60 minutes

Chapter 1: The Dream

From Email to Call

"Last time you arranged a meeting by email — now answer the phone. What do you say first?"
"Do you prefer calls, video, or email at work? Why?"
"What is hard for you on an English call?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1Phone & Video Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2Answering, Holding & Polite Requests (Can/Could)
3Join a Video Call with a Partner
4Practice — make and answer calls
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To return a phone call
To call back
To return a phone call later
"I will call you back this afternoon."
To wait on the line
To hold on
To wait on the line for a moment
"Could you hold on a moment, please?"
Where you leave a recorded message
Voicemail
A system to leave a recorded message
"I left a message on her voicemail."
To turn your microphone off
To mute
To turn your microphone off
"Please mute your microphone when you are not speaking."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To turn your microphone back on
To unmute
To turn your microphone back on
"Let me unmute. Can you hear me now?"
How well your phone or internet works
Connection
How well your phone or internet works
"Sorry, the connection is bad."
To end a phone call
To hang up
To end a phone call
"Let us not hang up until we finish."
To press the numbers to make a call
To dial
To press the numbers to make a call
"I dial the number to call the office."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Use It in a Sentence

To call back
To hold on
Voicemail
To mute
To unmute
Connection
To hang up
To dial
Chapter 3: The Code

The Language of
a Call

Grammar Discovery

Phone Phrases

"Hello, this is Rafael speaking."
"Could I speak to Ms. Collins?"
"Could you hold on a moment?"
"Can you hear me now?"
StepPhrase
AnswerHello, this is + name + speaking.
Ask for someoneCould I speak to + name?
Ask to waitCould you hold on a moment?
Check audioCan you hear me?
Grammar Recap

Can & Could on the Phone

FunctionPhone phrase
Ability / audio check"Can you hear me?"
Polite request"Could you call back later?"
Ask to wait"Could you hold on, please?"
Offer help"Can I take a message?"
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"Who are you?" (answering the phone)
"Who is calling, please?"
"Wait!" (asking someone to hold)
"Could you hold on a moment, please?"

Use polite phone phrases — not direct commands.

Grammar Practice

Complete the Call

"Hello, this is Rafael ___speaking." (answer)
"___Could I speak to Ms. Collins?" (ask for someone)
"Could you ___hold on a moment?" (ask to wait)
"You are on mute. Let me ___unmute." (video call)
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"Who are you?" (on the phone)
"Who is calling, please?" (polite phone phrase)
"I will call back you tomorrow."
"I will call you back tomorrow." (call + person + back)
"Can you to hold on, please?"
"Could you hold on, please?" (no 'to' after could)
"The connection are bad."
"The connection is bad." (singular subject)
0 / 4
Chapter 4: Getting There

A Video Call
with Sarah

Dialogue

The Video Call

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

Why could Sarah not hear Rafael at first?
He was on mute.
What does Rafael ask Sarah to do while he opens the file?
To hold on a moment.
What will Rafael do if the call drops?
Call her back right away.
Meeting Invite

The Video Conference

Video Conference
Grupo Picunha × Williams & Associates
Topic: Contract Review
Time: Thursday, 3:00 PM (BRT)
Platform: Zoom — link in your email
Please join with your camera on and mic on mute.
GRUPO PICUNHA

Answer the Phone

Your phone rings — it is a partner. Answer professionally and take the call. Use the keywords below.
this is... speakingcould I speak tohold oncan I helpcall back

Join a video call with a bad connection. Fix the audio and continue. Use only the keywords.
can you hear memuteunmuteconnectionhold on
Free Role-play

Make the Whole Call

Free: No support — just call!

Call an American partner: introduce yourself, ask to speak to the right person, make one request, and end the call politely. No keywords — just you.
Your Toolkit

5 Phone Phrases You Always Need

1
Hello, this is Rafael Gasparello Lima speaking.
2
Could I speak to Ms. Collins, please?
3
Could you hold on a moment, please?
4
Sorry, the connection is bad. Could you call back?
5
Can you hear me now? I will unmute.
What I Learned

Lesson 8 Checklist

I can answer the phone professionally
I can ask to speak to someone
I can ask someone to hold and make polite requests
I can handle audio problems on a video call
I can leave and respond to a voicemail

Lesson 8 — Complete

Lesson 9 — Rafael Presents the Figures

Numbers
and Data

Reports & Figures — A2 — 60 minutes

Chapter 1: The Dream

Time for the Numbers

"On the video call from last time — now present the figures. Where does your revenue come from?"
"How do you say this number: 1,500,000?"
"Which division do you think grew more this year?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1Data Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2Reading Numbers & Comparatives
3Present the Quarterly Figures
4Practice — compare and report
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

When a number goes up
Increase
When a number goes up
"Revenue showed a strong increase."
When a number goes down
Decrease
When a number goes down
"Agriculture had a small decrease."
Money the company keeps after costs
Profit
Money the company keeps after costs
"Profit is higher than last year."
When something gets bigger over time
Growth
When something gets bigger over time
"The iron division had the highest growth."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A period of three months
Quarter
A period of three months
"Revenue grew this quarter."
A guess about future numbers
Forecast
A prediction about future numbers
"We forecast steady growth."
A number, especially in a report
Figure
A number, especially in a report
"All the figures are in the report."
A part of 100 (%)
Percentage
A part of one hundred (%)
"Revenue grew by twelve percent."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Use It in a Sentence

Increase
Decrease
Profit
Growth
Quarter
Forecast
Figure
Percentage
Chapter 3: The Code

Reading & Comparing
Numbers

Grammar Discovery

Comparing Figures

"Profit is higher than last year."
"Iron grew faster than agriculture."
"Iron had the highest growth."
"This quarter is better than the last one."
CompareForm
Two thingsadjective + -er than: higher than
Long adjectivemore + adjective + than
The top onethe + adjective + -est: the highest
Irregulargood → better / the best
Grammar Recap

Saying Numbers

WrittenYou say
1,500,000one million, five hundred thousand
12%twelve percent
7.5%seven point five percent
+12% / -3%up twelve percent / down three percent
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"Profit is more high than last year."
"Profit is higher than last year."
"Revenue increased of twelve percent."
"Revenue increased by twelve percent."

Short adjectives: add -er (not "more"). Increase/decrease + by.

Grammar Practice

Complete the Report

"Revenue ___increased by twelve percent." (went up)
"Profit is ___higher than last year." (comparative)
"Iron had the ___highest growth." (superlative)
"Agriculture ___decreased by three percent." (went down)
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"Profit is more high than last year."
"Profit is higher than last year." (short adjective + -er)
"Revenue increased of twelve percent."
"Revenue increased by twelve percent." (increase + by)
"Iron had the most high growth."
"Iron had the highest growth." (the + -est)
"This quarter is gooder than the last."
"This quarter is better than the last." (good → better)
0 / 4
Chapter 4: Getting There

Presenting the
Q1 Figures

Dialogue

The Quarterly Review

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

By what percentage did revenue grow?
Twelve percent.
Which division had the highest growth?
The iron division.
What is Rafael's forecast for next quarter?
Steady growth — even better than this quarter.
Quarterly Report

Q1 Figures

Q1 Report — Grupo Picunha
First Quarter Results
Total revenue: +12% vs last quarter
Iron division: +18% (highest growth)
Agriculture division: -3%
Profit: higher than last year
FORECAST Q2: STEADY GROWTH
Listening 1

The Presentation

Listen first — what are the key numbers?

0:000:00
Speed
Listening 2

The Questions

Listen first — what does she ask about?

0:000:00
Speed
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
Revenue went up 12%. How do you say it?
"Revenue increased by twelve percent."
1 / 6
Quick Fire

Keep Going

0 / 6
Compare two figures: 20% and 12%. Which is bigger?
"Twenty percent is higher than twelve percent."
1 / 6

12/12 — Excellent!

Read figures, compare divisions, and forecast with confidence.

Guided Role-play

Present the Quarter

Guided: Use the keywords

Present your Q1 results to a partner. Use the figures from the report and the keywords below.
increased byhighest growthprofithigher thanforecast
Semi-free Role-play

Compare the Divisions

Semi-free: Only keywords

Compare the iron and agriculture divisions for a partner. Use only the keywords.
faster thanthe highestdecreasedpercentquarter
Free Role-play

Your Own Report

Free: No support — just present!

Present a short quarterly report to an American partner: give the main figures, compare the two divisions, mention profit, and give a forecast. No keywords — just you.
Your Toolkit

5 Data Phrases You Always Need

1
Revenue increased by twelve percent this quarter.
2
The iron division had the highest growth.
3
Profit is higher than last year.
4
Agriculture decreased by three percent.
5
We forecast steady growth for the next quarter.
What I Learned

Lesson 9 Checklist

I can say large numbers and percentages
I can describe an increase and a decrease
I can compare two figures (higher than)
I can use superlatives (the highest)
I can give a simple forecast

Lesson 9 — Complete

Next: Lesson 10 — "At the Airport: Travel Essentials"

Homework: Prepare a 1-minute summary of your real quarterly figures.

Lesson 10 — Rafael Flies to New York

At the
Airport

Travel Essentials — A2 — 60 minutes

Chapter 1: The Dream

Ready to Fly

"Read this aloud: departure at 3:40, gate 22. (numbers from last time)"
"You travel to the US once a year — what is hard at the airport?"
"What must you always show at check-in?"
Today's Plan

Your Agenda

1Airport Vocabulary — 8 essential words
2Rules & Advice — must, have to, should, can
3Check In for a Flight to New York
4Practice — check-in, security, boarding
Vocabulary

Words 1-4

The card that lets you get on the plane
Boarding pass
The card that lets you get on the plane
"Here is my passport and boarding pass."
The door where you get on the plane
Gate
The door where you board the plane
"Your flight departs from gate twenty-two."
Where you register for your flight
Check-in
Where you register for your flight
"How many bags do you have for check-in?"
Your bags and suitcases
Luggage
Your bags and suitcases
"Can I take this luggage on board?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

When the plane leaves
Departure
When the plane leaves
"Departure is at three forty."
Where officials check what you bring into a country
Customs
Where officials check what you bring into a country
"You have to go through customs."
A seat next to the walking path
Aisle seat
A seat next to the walking path on the plane
"Could I have an aisle seat, please?"
When something is late
Delay
When the flight is late
"Is there any delay with the flight?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary Review

Use It in a Sentence

Boarding pass
Gate
Check-in
Luggage
Departure
Customs
Aisle seat
Delay
Chapter 3: The Code

Rules & Advice
at the Airport

Grammar Discovery

Modal Verbs

"You must show your passport."
"You have to go through security."
"You should arrive early."
"You can't bring large liquids."
ModalMeaning
must / have toobligation (a rule): you must / you have to
shouldadvice (a good idea): you should
canpermission / possibility: you can
can't / mustn'tnot allowed: you can't / you mustn't
Grammar Recap

Rules, Advice & Permission

At the airportModal phrase
Rule"You must be at the gate on time."
Obligation"You have to show your boarding pass."
Advice"You should check that big bag."
Permission"Can I take this on board?"
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"You must to show your passport."
"You must show your passport."
"I have to checking in my bag."
"I have to check in my bag."

After must / should / can: base verb (no "to", no -ing).

Grammar Practice

Complete the Rules

"You ___must show your passport." (rule)
"You ___have to go through security." (obligation)
"You ___should check that big bag." (advice)
"___Can I take this on board?" (permission)
Spot the Error

Find the Mistake

"You must to show your passport."
"You must show your passport." (no 'to' after must)
"I have to checking in my bag."
"I have to check in my bag." (base verb)
"Can I to have an aisle seat?"
"Can I have an aisle seat?" (no 'to' after can)
"The flight have a delay."
"The flight has a delay." (it + has)
0 / 4
Chapter 4: Getting There

At the
Check-in Desk

Dialogue

The Check-in

Comprehension

Did You Catch That?

What seat does Rafael ask for?
An aisle seat.
What gate and time is the departure?
Gate twenty-two, at three forty.
Is there a delay?
No, the flight is on time.
Boarding Pass

Rafael's Flight

Boarding Pass
Passenger: Rafael Gasparello Lima
Flight GP 204 — São Paulo → New York
Gate 22 — Seat 14C (aisle)
Departure: 3:40 PM — Boarding: 3:10 PM
Status: On time
GRUPO PICUNHA — BUSINESS TRIP
Listening 1

An Announcement

Listen first — gate, time, and delay?

0:000:00
Speed
Listening 2

Rafael's Travel Routine

Listen first — what does he always do?

0:000:00
Speed
Quick Fire

Quick Fire

0 / 6
At check-in, the agent asks for documents. What do you show?
"Here is my passport and boarding pass."
1 / 6
Quick Fire

Keep Going

0 / 6
The agent advises you to check a big bag. How does she say it?
"You should check it in."
1 / 6

12/12 — Excellent!

Check in, follow the rules, and board with confidence.

Guided Role-play

Check In for Your Flight

Guided: Use the keywords

You are at the check-in desk for your flight to New York. Check in your bag and choose a seat. Use the keywords below.
passportboarding passcheck-inaisle seatgate
Semi-free Role-play

A Small Problem

Semi-free: Only keywords

Your bag is too big and there is a delay. Ask the agent what you must and should do. Use only the keywords.
delayluggageshouldhave tocustoms
Free Role-play

Your Whole Journey

Free: No support — just travel!

Take a full trip through the airport: check in, ask one question, follow the rules (security and customs), and get to your gate. No keywords — just you.
Your Toolkit

5 Airport Phrases You Always Need

1
Here is my passport and boarding pass.
2
Can I take this luggage on board?
3
Could I have an aisle seat, please?
4
I must be at the gate before departure.
5
Is there any delay with the flight?
What I Learned

Lesson 10 Checklist

I can check in and show my documents
I can ask about seats, gates, and delays
I can use must and have to for rules
I can use should for advice and can for permission
I know the airport steps: check-in, security, customs, gate

Lesson 10 — Complete
